coredata.dc:description: Upgrades to power systems and the rapid growth of electric vehicles significantly heighten the importance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) in energy systems. As a complex dynamic system, the charging and discharging process of LiBs involves the evolution of multiphysics fields, such as concentration, electricity, and stress. For quantitative analysis of the internal mechanisms of LiBs, as well as the development guidance and performance prediction of high-performance batteries, modeling has advantages that cannot be matched by traditional experimental methods. Major research efforts in the past decades have made significant strides in modeling the internal processes and physical field evolution of LiBs. Importantly, the scattered ideas need to be integrated into a structured framework to form a complete LiBs multi-physical field model. This work reviews important advances in LiBs modeling from the perspectives of describing the internal processes of the battery and portraying the evolution of the physical field. First, quantitative descriptions of the charging and discharging behaviors and the side reactions are reviewed to investigate the battery reaction mechanisms. In addition, the characterization of the evolution of the stress and temperature fields within the battery as well as the coupling between them and the internal reactions are discussed. Finally, some suggestions for future improvements in the modeling are given, ranging from equation optimization to parameter acquisition and the application of artificial intelligence. It is hoped that this work will facilitate the development of models with sufficient accuracy and efficient computational cost to provide guidance for the improvement of LiBs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Upgrades to power systems and the rapid growth of electric vehicles significantly heighten the importance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) in energy systems. As a complex dynamic system, the charging and discharging process of LiBs involves the evolution of multiphysics fields, such as concentration, electricity, and stress. For quantitative analysis of the internal mechanisms of LiBs, as well as the development guidance and performance prediction of high-performance batteries, modeling has advantages that cannot be matched by traditional experimental methods. Major research efforts in the past decades have made significant strides in modeling the internal processes and physical field evolution of LiBs. Importantly, the scattered ideas need to be integrated into a structured framework to form a complete LiBs multi-physical field model. This work reviews important advances in LiBs modeling from the perspectives of describing the internal processes of the battery and portraying the evolution of the physical field. First, quantitative descriptions of the charging and discharging behaviors and the side reactions are reviewed to investigate the battery reaction mechanisms. In addition, the characterization of the evolution of the stress and temperature fields within the battery as well as the coupling between them and the internal reactions are discussed. Finally, some suggestions for future improvements in the modeling are given, ranging from equation optimization to parameter acquisition and the application of artificial intelligence. It is hoped that this work will facilitate the development of models with sufficient accuracy and efficient computational cost to provide guidance for the improvement of LiBs.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of modern power systems, efficient energy acquisition and storage technologies have become the focus of social attention [1]. Simultaneously, to meet the power supply needs of various new devices such as electric vehicles, mobile phones, flexible electronics and so on, exploit and develop low-cost, efficient, clean and renewable energy conversion and storage system has become an urgent priority [2]. To date, rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) have risen to prominence as the most prevalent power sources for portable and mobile applications due to their reasonable energy density, rate capability, and cycle life [3–6]. However, despite their widespread adoption, limitations such as the insufficient theoretical capacity of both the cathode and anode, capacity fading issues, low initial coulombic efficiency (CE) and safety hazards impede their widespread application in next-generation energy storage systems [4,7–10]. Hence, there is an urgent need to explore and develop high-performance electrode materials and novel electrode structures to improve the capabilities of lithium-ion battery technology and to realize the full potential of LiBs as a critical step to meet the growing demands of modern energy storage applications.

LiBs are complex, dynamic systems with continuously evolving composition, microstructure, and properties, and the general structure is shown in Fig. 1 a [11]. It is generally understood that the charging and discharging behaviors of LiBs are achieved by solid-phase and liquid-phase mass transfer coupled with interfacial reactions. In addition, a series of side reactions occur during the process, as well as changes in physical fields such as temperature, stress, electricity, and concentration, and there is a complex coupling between these reactions and the evolution of the fields [12]. Over the past decades, numerous notable studies have qualitatively and quantitatively explored the intrinsic mechanisms of reactions and the evolution of physical fields using advanced characterization techniques [13–15], providing guidance for the enhancement of LiBs through insights into material properties and electrode structure design [14,16–18]. In research, the study of the mechanism and prediction of battery performance through modeling is crucial [19]. Modeling allows for vivid descriptions of the internal processes of a battery, detailed analysis of underlying mechanisms, and accurate performance evaluation. The parameters within the model quantify the effects of material properties and spatial structure on battery performance, as well as their interrelationships, providing guidance for targeted battery optimization [20–23].

Fig. 1b illustrates a series of processes that occur within a battery during charging and discharging [24]. As mentioned earlier, the charge/discharge behaviors of the battery are achieved by the movement and de-embedding of lithium ions between the positive and negative electrodes. Among them, the lithium-ion intercalation reaction is described by charge transfer kinetics [25,26], and the transfer of ions and electrons is portrayed by the mass transfer equation [27]. Generation and regeneration of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) and lithium planting are two of the most dominant side reactions in LiBs, the reaction rates of which are usually described using interfacial reaction kinetics [28,29]. All of these behaviors have complex coupling relationships with each other and with the concentration, electricity, temperature, and stress fields within the cell. As a result, they are difficult to accurately describe using traditional simulation methods and must be examined across multiphysics fields to gain deeper insight into the electrochemical mechanisms within the cell [24,30].

As early as 1962, Newman et al. [31–33] started a series of studies on the modeling of batteries. In 1993, they proposed an electrochemical model of LiBs, i.e., the pseudo-two-dimensions (P2D) model, which is still one of the most widely used models for LiBs today [34]. In recent decades, researchers worldwide have shown great interest in the multiphysics modeling of LiBs, and a series of modeling studies at different scales have been performed, including but not limited to mechanistic investigations, performance prediction, and guidance for battery improvement, which provide powerful tools for the development of the next-generation LiBs [35–39]. Fang et al. [40] proposed a two-dimensional heterogeneous particle stacking model and used it to guide the microstructural design of electrode structures for LiBs, pointing out that multilayered electrode structures with different porosities can effectively improve the rate performance of the cell. Ansah et al. [41] studied the effect of particle size and porosity of the LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2 (NMC) cathode on the performance of LiBs through a model, providing guidance for the optimization of battery performance. For various multiphysics models of LiBs, researchers have also developed a range of simulation software packages, such as COMSOL Multiphysics, fullCellFoam [42], batP2dFoam [38], PETLION [43], LIONSIMBA [44], Pybamm [45], DUALFOIL [46] and DEARLIBS [47]. These programs provide a rich variety of methods for solving models. In terms of summarizing models, a series of review articles have also appeared in recent years. Ali et al. [19] reviewed a series of models describing the aging of LiBs and proposed a combined multi-physics field and data-based modeling framework to model and validate multi-scale aging effects. Mehta et al. [48] summarise the various physically-based step-down, equivalent circuit, and data-driven models that have been developed over the years to capture the electrochemical, degradation and thermal response of LiBs, and discuss the main challenges and research directions for the development of high-fidelity battery health management systems. Rojas et al. [49] reviewed parameter selection and acquisition in electrochemical modeling, proposed a hybrid parametric approach combining invasive and non-invasive techniques, and discussed how to obtain a reliable set of physical parameters.

In this review, we present key elements and recent advances in multiphysics field modeling and development under the continuous medium hypothesis for LiBs, focusing on the description of the physicochemical processes inside the batteries as well as on the portrayal of the evolution of the multiphysics field. Firstly, we summarise our approach to modeling the charging and discharging processes of LiBs, including lithium-ion diffusion in the electrode particles, lithium-ion migration in the electrolyte, charge conservation in the cell, and the calculation of interfacial reaction rates. We then describe how to inscribe the side reactions inside the battery, including the generation and regeneration of SEI and lithium planting/stripping, as well as their effects on the loss of battery capacity and increased internal resistance. Next, we discuss the description of the evolution of the internal stress and temperature fields during battery charging and discharging, as well as the portrayal of the complex coupling between physical field variations and internal reactions. Finally, we identify existing challenges in LiB modeling and propose what the authors consider to be promising solutions. The purpose of this work is to highlight the contributions of the scientific community in modeling and simulation and to integrate the scattered ideas from modeling works into a structured framework that will eventually lead to a complete multiphysics field model of LiBs, which will provide a powerful tool for the future development of advanced LiBs with satisfactory energy densities and lifetimes.

The schematic diagram of LiBs is shown in Fig. 1a, and its working principle is roughly as follows: during charging, lithium ions are de-embedded from the positive material and pass through the separator into the negative region, where they are then inserted into or react with the negative material. During discharge, all the electrochemical processes proceed in the reverse direction [50]. In this section, we describe the charge and discharge behavior of the battery from three perspectives: mass transfer, charge conservation, and interface reaction.

When the battery undergoes an interfacial reaction, lithium ions are transferred to the surface of the electrode particles through the electrolyte solution, and electrons are transferred to the surface of the particles through the electrode particles and the conductive agent. As is known, the Butler-Volmer Equation was developed based on the phenomenology of traditional electrochemical reactions with inert electrodes and solvated ions in electrolyte solutions [55]. However, it may be not appropriate for solid-liquid ion-intercalation reactions on electrode surfaces (Fig. 2 a) [56]. According to Munakata et al. [57], the Tafel curve is severely curved at high overpotentials, as shown in Fig. 2b, which indicates that the interfacial reaction rate at the electrode is limited by mass transfer. In response to the phenomenon of bending of the Tafel curve, Bazant et al. [55,58] proposed the use of the M-H-C integral [59–61] to calculate the interfacial reaction rate:

Through the M-H-C model, Peng et al. [55] successfully calculated the interfacial reaction rates at different overpotentials, achieving good agreement with the experimental data, as shown in Fig. 2c. Researchers are beginning to embrace the use of M-H-C theory to calculate interfacial reaction rates [63,64]. Zhao et al. [62] calculated the exchange current density and homogeneous free energy for the LiFePO4 reaction based on Marcus theory, as shown in Fig. 2d. However, such accuracy comes at a cost, as calculating the interfacial reaction rate using the M-H-C model takes four times as long as the B–V equation in the same model [55].

During battery charging and discharging, a series of reactions occur on the surface of the electrode particles. As shown in Fig. 3 , graphite particles involve a series of side reactions including lithium plating/stripping and (re)formation of SEIs in addition to lithium-ion (de)embedding during charging and discharging. These side effects ultimately lead to a decrease in the capacity and power of the battery [65]. Lithium dendrites are formed during plating/stripping, inducing continuous electrolyte degradation/consumption and thus continuous (re)formation of the SEI [66]. In turn, the regrowth of the SEI leads to changes in the internal potential of the cell, promoting lithium precipitation [67].

Clearly, there is a complex coupling between the above aging mechanisms, and the contribution of a single mechanism to aging is difficult to determine. However, this complex coupling relationship has been neglected in many past works, which only studied the battery aging caused by a certain side reaction [68–72].

The SEI is a complex inhomogeneous disordered passivation layer formed in situ on a negative electrode by electrolyte decomposition [73–75]. While an intact and stable SEI can limit electron tunneling and prevent electrolyte reduction to maintain the (electro)chemical stability of the battery, an evolving SEI can continue to deplete the electrolyte and active lithium ions, leading to increased battery resistance, capacity degradation, and low power density and ultimately facilitating thermal runaway events that lead to battery failure [76–79]. Throughout the life cycle of a battery, performance degradation is closely related to the degradation of the electrolyte [80]. It is important to note that the addition of excess electrolytes can lead to reduced energy density and increased battery costs. Therefore, an accurate assessment of electrolyte consumption during battery aging is essential for determining the optimal initial electrolyte amount [81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrolyte decomposition and components of the SEI
ce:section.ce:para: The SEI generation process involves a series of electrochemical reactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface, resulting in the precipitation of various organic and inorganic compounds [82]. Atalay et al. [83]incorporated the diffusion coefficients of lithium ions, ethylene carbonate (EC), and ethylene carbonate (DMC) as functions of local porosity. They determined the consumed electrolyte volume based on the volume fraction of solvent components participating in side reactions. Researchers usually use the rate of SEI generation as a substitute for the rate of electrolyte decomposition [84].

The mechanisms for its generation are usually considered to be the decomposition of the electrolyte and the reaction of the electrolyte solution with the lithium salt [85]. Over the past few decades, advanced characterization techniques have been employed to gain deeper insights into the structure and composition of the SEI [86–90]. These techniques enable researchers to study the SEI at the molecular level, providing valuable information that can guide the development of more effective SEI optimization strategies. By analyzing the results of the characterization, the composition of the SEI and its generation process can be roughly deduced, and a schematic diagram is obtained, as shown in Fig. 4 . To date, however, the specific components, formation processes, and functions of SEIs remain inconclusive, although researchers have reached some consensus on some aspects of SEI components and production processes [91].

Based on current research advances, temperature variations can lead to different reaction pathways during SEI formation, resulting in layers with distinct physical and chemical properties. For instance, higher temperatures may accelerate the decomposition of the electrolyte, leading to a thicker and potentially less stable SEI [92–94]. Conversely, lower temperatures might result in a thinner SEI that could offer less protection against electrolyte decomposition [95]. The choice of electrolyte also plays a crucial role, as different solvents and salts can decompose to form diverse SEI components [96,97]. Additionally, the cathode and anode materials influence the types of reactions that occur at the interface, thereby affecting the structure and composition of the SEI [98–101].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation and regeneration of the SEI
ce:section.ce:para:
In the works described above, researchers now have a deeper understanding of the composition and formation mechanisms of the SEI, but the computational demands of modeling a complete picture of their internal mechanisms far outweigh the value they can bring. As a result, researchers now often use one or two homogenized interfacial reaction rates in their modeling to simplify the generation of SEIs [37,67,103]. Li et al. [28] proposed an SEI formation model based on electron tunneling [104] for the special structure of an SEI with a dense inner layer and a porous outer layer. The bilayer structure of the SEI is considered in the model to be generated by two processes during (dis)charging and at rest, which are shown schematically in Fig. 5 a and b. The probability of electron tunneling is expressed as

Computing the SEI generation by electronic tunneling is indeed a sufficiently accurate method, but excessive complexity can place large demands on computing power. Therefore, calculating the SEI generation rate by simple Tafel kinetics has become one of the more commonly used current methods [84]. As early as 2001, Wang et al. [105] measured the increase in the internal resistance of a battery with an increase in the number of cycles, as shown in Fig. 5c. Tahmasbi et al. [106] proposed a method to simulate the growth of the SEI based on side reactions while revealing the increase in the internal resistance of the battery with increasing SEI growth, and a schematic of the model is shown in Fig. 5d.

is the total current density.

The (re)generation of the SEI has a multidimensional impact on the performance of batteries. First, there is competition between the current of SEI growth and the current of the electrode intercalation reaction, as shown in Fig. 5d, so the growth of the SEI leads to a decrease in cell power. Second, as the number of cycles increases, the SEI on the surface of the electrode particles becomes progressively thicker, resulting in additional resistance, as shown in Fig. 5e. Ender et al. [108] proposed a modified soild-phase potential expression that describes this effect:
(18)

is the resistance of the SEI.

In addition, regeneration of the SEI leads to degradation of the battery capacity. Researchers have calculated the total amount of SEI generation by integrating the SEI generation rate to calculate the loss of battery capacity [83,109].

The deposition of lithium ions on the surface of a negative electrode during charging is known as lithium plating, which increases the risk of short-circuiting the battery [110]. In the event of failure of the equilibrium conditions for lithium deposition, the deposited lithium is stripped and re-enters the electrolyte [111]. However, as the volume of lithium decreases during the stripping process, some of the lithium loses its electrical connection to the negative electrode particles and becomes dead lithium, which can lead to a loss of recyclable lithium [29,72], a decrease in porosity, and an increase in ion diffusion impedance [48]. To inhibit the occurrence of lithium precipitation or reduce the production of dead lithium, various methods have been applied, including electrolyte solvent selection [112–114], lithium salts [70,71,115,116], the addition of additives [117–120], and applying pressure [121–123].

Arora et al. [124] presented the first model for lithium deposition in LiMn2O4/C batteries. Subsequently, the Arora model was extended and simplified by Newman et al. [125] and Perkins et al. [126], respectively. In the above study, the Li deposition current was related to the Li reaction potential through the B–V equation. As the study progressed, a greater number of thermodynamic and kinetic factors were considered to describe the lithium deposition reaction [125,127,128]. These included the voltage of the graphite and the concentration of lithium in the solid and liquid phases [124,129,130]. However, a description of lithium stripping is still lacking in these works. Chen et al. [131,132] implemented a coupled B–V equation phase field model for Li dendrite growth based on electrochemical phase field theory [133]. Zhang et al. [134] simulated the Li planting and stripping process through a phase field model and visualized the dead lithium generation process (Fig. 6 a).

Since only two phases, lithium metal and electrolyte, exist in the phase field model, which is not a complete battery structure, the specific equations are not discussed in detail in the article. Lithium deposition and stripping are described in the complete cell model using a combined thermodynamic and kinetic lithium deposition-stripping model proposed by Duan et al. [135] The lithium deposition and lithium peeling processes they described are shown in Fig. 6b. The equilibrium potentials of lithium deposition and stripping

Similar to the effect of the SEI, the effects of lithium planting on battery performance can be divided into two areas. First, since there is also competition between the current of the generation of dead lithium and the current of the electrode intercalation reaction, this competition also leads to a decrease in battery power. Second, the presence of dead lithium leads to the loss of lithium inventory in the cell, which results in a decrease in cell capacity, and this effect can be calculated by integrating the deposition rate of dead lithium.

After years of continuous improvement of the graphite negative electrode by researchers, the available specific capacity of the graphite negative electrode is very close to its theoretical specific capacity (372 mAh g−1), and the development of the graphite negative electrode has reached a bottleneck [136]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find an anode with a larger specific capacity to replace traditional graphite anodes to further increase the battery energy density. Therefore, silicon anodes, which have an ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity of 4200 mAh g−1, have been investigated by researchers [137]. However, silicon negative electrodes have serious drawbacks: the volume of silicon expands by more than 300 ​% during lithiation and delithiation [138]. Under such drastic volume changes, the SEI membrane on the surface of the electrode is constantly ruptured and regenerated [1], and even the electrode itself breaks down [14], which ultimately leads to severe capacity degradation and very short battery life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation and transfer of internal stresses
ce:section.ce:para: Because silicon anodes have a sufficiently high theoretical specific capacity, researchers have begun to try to overcome their dramatic volume change to use them in batteries [14,16]. First, accurate detection of the stress changes inside the cell as well as the distribution of the stress field is necessary. Researchers have developed a series of stress detection devices and corresponding procedures to enable the detection and analysis of stresses inside a battery [139–145]. After obtaining the distribution of the stresses, it is necessary to explore the damage mechanism of the stresses on the cell [67,81]. Furthermore, based on the understanding of the generation and damage mechanisms, it is possible to predict the generation of internal stresses in a battery and assess the risk of damage to the battery [81]. In the series of studies described above, modeling calculations have been shown to be vastly superior in terms of concretely understanding the damage mechanisms of internal stresses as well as predicting stress generation and assessing cell damage.

In the anode of a lithium-ion battery, nanoscale particles (primary particles) of active material form secondary particles by agglomeration, and the tight arrangement of the secondary particles ultimately forms the electrode. The generation of intracellular stresses therefore involves multiple scales from individual particles to cells, and modeling of intracellular stresses from the smallest to the largest scales will be reviewed here. Newman et al. [146] described the generation of internal stresses in individual particles during lithiation and the fragmentation of the particles by means of physical modeling, as shown in Fig. 7 a. Iqbal et al. [147] also presented a model that portrays the stress generation during lithiation for a negative electrode particle with a binder attached to its surface. Purkayastha et al. [148] presented a model describing the stresses generated in a cubic particle when lithium is embedded.

The generation of internal stresses in the particles is described as:

Due to the close arrangement of the electrode particles, the expansion of a single particle squeezes the surrounding particles, leading to the generation of interparticle stresses. Gao et al. [149] proposed a model for calculating interparticle extrusion, which is shown schematically in Fig. 7b. The source of stress is defined in the model as the expansion of silicon particles when embedded in lithium, and the stress on the contact surface of the two particles is calculated by means of an elastoplastic contact model. For the secondary particles formed by particle agglomeration of the electrode active material, Wu et al. [150] proposed a model, as shown in Fig. 7c, to calculate the stress changes on the secondary particle scale. The model sets the radius difference between primary and secondary particles to one order of magnitude to ensure the mechanical homogeneity of secondary particles. Both primary and secondary particles generate contact stresses when they come into contact with other particles when expansion occurs, and strain-stress cross-scale transfer is achieved from the expansion behavior within the particles to the interparticle stress response. The stress generated by the elastic contact of the particles can be expressed as:

represents the tiny strain due to particle extrusion and is the key factor in determining the level of stress.

When the active particles in the electrode undergo volume changes during charging and discharging, these localized expansions accumulate and translate into macroscopic deformation of the whole electrode, causing stress changes. This stress change may lead to cracking and delamination of the electrode, affecting its structural integrity and electrochemical performance. Therefore, understanding the transition from particle expansion to electrode deformation is crucial for improving the cycling stability of batteries. On this basis, Gao et al. [151,152] proposed a three-dimensional model called the RVE model for calculating the internal stresses in batteries, as shown in Fig. 7d. In this model, the RVE is used instead of the electrode as the target of the study, and the stress is transferred by squeezing between the particles. In practical calculations, the electrochemical-mechanical model can be constructed by transferring two parameters, concentration, and static stress, to reduce the computational effort. When the particles undergo expansion, squeezing occurs between neighboring particles, and the electrode space structure changes, the stress and strain at the electrode can be given by the following equation:

By varying the position of different particles in the model, it is possible to predict changes in performance due to changes in the spatial structure of the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rupture of the SEI and fragmentation of electrode particles
ce:section.ce:para: The expansion of electrode active material particles and the generation of stresses on the electrodes can have a multidimensional effect on battery performance. Zhang et al. [139] altered the stress inside a battery by externally applying pressure and found that the battery capacity retention decreases as the stress increases. The main reason for this phenomenon is that as the active particles in the electrodes undergo drastic volume changes during cycling, the particles themselves break up and lose physical contact with the collector [147], which simultaneously leads to the rupture and regeneration of the SEI [153], affecting ion and electron transport within the cell. In addition, an increase in the internal resistance of the cell and a decrease in the amount of effective active material directly lead to a decrease in the electrochemical performance of the cell [154,155].

Dramatic volume changes in silicon anode particles can increase the stability of surface SEIs. As cycling proceeds, SEIs continue to break off and regenerate on the silicon surface, resulting in increasing internal resistance and decreasing capacity of the cell [19,84]. Ali et al. [69] developed a coupled mechanical-electrochemical model of SEI fragmentation at the surface of the negative electrode of a battery using a core-shell model similar to the one shown in Fig. 7b to calculate the stresses on the SEI at the surface as the particles expand. The fracture of the SEI was calculated from the elastic strain energy
G

is the maximum tangential stress in the SEI layer.

Ding et al. [156] quantitatively investigated the failure time of inhomogeneous SEIs on the surface of silicon negative electrodes by setting up defects and investigated the failure time of SEIs at different electrode particle radii as well as at different charging and discharging multiplicities, improving rational design strategies for building a stable SEI.

Zhang et al. [139] reported that the expansion of a silicon negative electrode in a flexible pack cell leads to the expansion of the cell as a whole. Li et al. [157] investigated cell swelling due to the expansion of active material particles by moving the grid and investigated the effect of different silicon contents in the negative electrode and different porosities on the utilization of the active material. For batteries packaged in a robust case, expansion on the cell scale is not allowed, which means that the electrodes will be subjected to greater stresses during cycling. Li et al. [140] achieved an uneven stress distribution inside a cell by applying an uneven external force to the cell and observed via SEM that the electrodes were more prone to fragmentation after cycling in parts with higher stresses. As the fragmented part of the electrode loses physical contact with the collector, the electrons lack a transmission channel, and this part of the electrode will lose the ability to charge and discharge, resulting in a loss of battery capacity [158]. Particle fragmentation is described similarly to SEI rupture and is assessed by calculating its elastic strain energy [159]. The capacity loss due to electrode rupture is usually described by the change in electrode porosity, the evolution of which is calculated from the average active material loss rate [160].

In addition, the stresses inside the battery will also provide much information. By analyzing how the stresses change during charging and discharging, the state of health of the battery can be predicted [161]. The technique of estimating the SOC of a battery by detecting its stress is also now maturing, providing new insights into the SOC estimation of soft-packed LiBs [162].

Temperature significantly impacts the performance, longevity, and safety of batteries, making it a critical factor in the design and operation of battery-powered devices [12,163]. Both high and low temperatures can have detrimental effects on batteries, influencing their electrochemical reactions, capacity, internal resistance, and overall efficiency [100,164,165].

At high temperatures, the rate of electrochemical reactions within the battery increases [100,164]. However, this can lead to instability and breakdown of the SEI, resulting in more cell capacity degradation and increased internal resistance [102,107]. Moreover, high temperatures can exacerbate the growth of dendrites, which are needle-like structures that can form on the anode, particularly in LiBs. Dendrites can pierce the separator, potentially causing short circuits that can lead to battery failure or even catastrophic events such as fires or explosions. Furthermore, thermal runaway (TR) is a significant risk at high temperatures [166,167]. This phenomenon occurs when the heat generated within the battery exceeds the rate at which it can be dissipated, leading to a rapid increase in temperature and potentially resulting in a self-sustaining exothermic reaction. TRs can cause batteries to catch fire or explode, posing serious safety hazards.

Conversely, low temperatures also present challenges for battery performance and longevity [128,165]. At lower temperatures, the mobility of ions within the electrolyte decreases, leading to reduced ionic conductivity. This impedes the flow of lithium ions between the anode and cathode, resulting in a lower battery capacity and reduced power output. The SEI layer can become overly rigid and brittle at low temperatures [69]. Low temperatures also affect the battery charge acceptance. Charging a battery at low temperatures can lead to lithium plating, where metallic lithium deposits on the surface of the anode rather than intercalating into the anode material [168]. This not only reduces the amount of available lithium for subsequent cycles and diminishes capacity but also increases the risk of dendrite formation. Like at high temperatures, these dendrites can pierce the separator and cause short circuits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation and transfer of heat within the battery
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, LiBs exhibit a range of internal behaviors during charging and discharging, and the vast majority of these behaviors include energy changes that result in heat generation and temperature changes within the battery. To analyze the generation of heat inside a battery and its effect on battery performance, electrochemical-thermal coupling (ETC) models based on porous electrode theory, concentrated solution theory and electrochemical kinetics have been widely used [169].

First, consider the heat production inside the battery. The heat generated inside a battery usually consists of three components, namely, the heat of reaction

Combined with the previously described sources of heat production and the energy conservation equation, the distribution and change in temperature inside the battery due to heat production can be calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in the internal behavior of the battery
ce:section.ce:para: As stated at the beginning of this chapter, temperature can have a very significant impact on battery performance. During cycling, batteries may suffer from thermal runaway at high temperatures, leading to serious safety issues. Current related works focus on considering interfacial reactions and current heating in models and providing TR early warning through temperature thresholds [171]. However, the appearance of the TR is usually due to the thermal aggregation caused by the inhomogeneity of the physical properties inside the battery, which is contrary to the assumptions of homogeneity and isotropy that are used in most of the models currently in use. Therefore, purely physical models cannot accurately describe the thermal runaway phenomenon due to the limitations of the current research level and arithmetic power. Nevertheless, with the developments in artificial intelligence (AI) and data-driven algorithms, predicting the risk of TR through machine learning has proven to be an effective methodology [172,173]. Das et al. [174] proposed a workflow, as shown in Fig. 8 , that combines physical modeling and machine learning to predict TR events. The heat production inside the battery is calculated by the P2D model and then simulated by the three-dimensional heat transfer model to obtain the temperature distribution inside the battery with some physical significance. On this basis, the data are analyzed by AI, and the battery is evaluated for the TR and partitioned according to the TR risk.

Furthermore, temperature affects a range of behaviors within the battery. Zhang et al. [175] considered the open-circuit voltage of the electrode as a function of temperature and performed a Taylor expansion of it near the reference temperature to obtain the variation in the open-circuit voltage of the electrode as a function of temperature for different SOCs. For all the reaction rate constants during interfacial reactions and the diffusion coefficients during mass transfer, the relationship with temperature can be described using the Arrhenius equation [176]:

For lithium precipitation in batteries, the equilibrium potential of the reaction can be calculated from equation (19), and the exchange current density of the reaction can be calculated from equation (39). Sun et al. [177,178] reported the promotion of lithium precipitation at low temperatures and reported that the addition of silicon particles to the negative electrode inhibits lithium precipitation in the negative electrode. The relationship between the exchange current density and temperature during the generation of the SEI can also be described by equation (37). For the generated SEI, too high or too low a temperature can lead to changes in the pore structure and the creation of many defect sites in the SEI layer [179]. In addition, changes in temperature lead to intrinsic strains in electrode particles [180],
(40)

In summary, an in-depth understanding of the various processes occurring in state-of-the-art battery systems, e.g., the transport of ions and electrons during discharge of LiBs as well as reactive processes at interfaces, the generation and regeneration of SEIs as well as lithium planting, and the variability of stress and temperature fields inside the batteries, needs to be visualized by multiphysics models. This paper reviews multiphysics models at multiple scales from primary particles to electrodes. In contrast to previous reviews that have focused primarily on continuous medium models, we concentrate on the modeling and simulation of detailed physical, chemical and electrochemical phenomena associated with discharge and charging processes, including ion transport in porous electrodes, electron migration, electrode reaction kinetics, generation and regeneration of SEIs, lithium planting, volume change phenomena, heat generation and outgassing, as well as coupling between the above behaviors. Undoubtedly, substantial challenges still remain for modeling LiBs. To develop more powerful predictive models that can be used for the rational use of high-performance LiBs, attention should be paid to the following aspects:

Firstly, a more accurate description of the internal behaviour of the battery is required. However, the balance between model accuracy and computational power requirement is an important issue. The Cahn-Hilliard theory can more accurately describe the phase separation in the electrode particles compared to Fick's law, but the fourth-order differential equations pose a great challenge to the solver; the M-H-C theory can better characterise the electrode-interface reaction rates compared to the B–V equations, but the four-fold computational requirement weakens the researchers' interest in it; the formation mechanism of SEI has been thoroughly investigated by means of characterisation, but its generation is still reduced to a single reaction for calculation in models, and SEI on the surface of electrode particles is usually treated as a homogeneous thin film to study its impact on cell performance. In future works, improving the accuracy of simplified models and optimising the computational difficulty of accurate models are important research directions.

Second, the reliability and accuracy of the input parameters are critical for modeling results. The values of many parameters may significantly affect the predicted performance, including electrolyte properties (e.g., thermodynamic diffusion coefficient), electrode geometry (e.g., specific surface area, porosity, tortuosity), and reaction kinetics (e.g., reaction rate constants). However, some of these parameters are often unavailable or incomplete, limiting the accuracy of battery performance prediction and evaluation. Efforts should be made to advance characterization and measurement techniques, particularly in situ, to provide accurate values for these important parameters.

Third, electrochemical modeling at the system scale needs to be considered. For cell, the distribution of temperature and stress, and the flow of electrolytes will greatly affect the battery performance and lifetime, and it is meaningless to portray the electrochemical behavior of the battery module through microscopic models such as the P2D model. However, the computational amount is a big problem for the three-dimensional macroscopic model that contains the complete information of the battery. Separating the stress-temperature model from the electrochemical model can be considered, and the coupling of the models can be achieved through the transfer of parameters. Additionally, the cross-scale transfer of stress and heat is an important research direction. Consideration can be given to using different scales of models for different research objects, and then achieving coupling through the transfer of parameters between models. In addition, cross-scale transfer of parameters is also an important research direction.

Fourth, multiphysics modeling can combined with AI. AI is a viable technique that can quickly provide reliable predictions that are reproducible and save computational time based on the statistical laws behind high-dimensional data. On the other hand, at the current level of research, the anisotropy of materials in batteries and the abrupt changes in performance due to positive feedback behavior are difficult to describe by conventional electrochemical models, at which point AI can be used to predict the probability of their occurrence and describe them in the form of stochastic parameters.

Considering the important role of modeling in battery mechanism exploration, performance prediction, and optimization guidance, it will be the cornerstone to drive the continued growth of battery technology and further development of the clean energy economy. We hope that this paper will help researchers in the electrochemical and battery communities to gain a comprehensive understanding of the current advances in modeling rechargeable batteries and to drive further technological advances in the near future.
